coredata.dc:title: Application of ZIF-67 coated by N-doped carbon dots in dispersive solid phase extraction of several pesticides from fruit juices and its combination with dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction followed by GC-FID
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Nazari Kalojeh, Mohammad
Hassanpour Sabet, Roghayeh
Farajzadeh, Mir Ali
Afshar Mogaddam, Mohammad Reza
Marzi Khosrowshahi, Elnaz
Tuzen, Mustafa
coredata.dc:description: A dispersive solid phase extraction procedure was established for the extraction of some pesticides from fruit juices and then it was combined with dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction for more enrichment of the analytes. For this purpose, 40 mg of zeolitic imidazolate framework-carbon dot composite was dispersed into the sample solution as the sorbent via vortex agitation. After that, the sorbent was collected by centrifugation and the supernatant was wasted. Then, 1 mL of a water-soluble organic solvent (acetone) was added onto the sorbent as the elution solvent. After desorption of the analytes from the sorbent surface, the eluate was mixed with a water-immiscible organic solvent (1,1,2-trichloro-1,2,2 trifluoroethane) at µL-level (45 µL) and the mixture was quickly injected into 5 mL deionized water. The resulting cloudy mixture was centrifuged and 1 µL of the sedimented phase was injected into a gas chromatography-flame ionization detection system. After the optimization of the method, its validation was evaluated. The data showed that the limits of detection and quantification obtained by this method were in the ranges of 0.22–0.72 and 0.73–2.40 ng mL−1, respectively. The plotted calibration curves were linear in the range of 0.73–1000 µg L−1 for all analytes. High enrichment factors (375−445), and extraction recoveries (75–89%), and negligible matrices effect were obtained for the investigated analytes by the introduced method. Also, the obtained data showed that all samples were free of the analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Dispersive solid phase extraction
Fruit juice
Metal-organic framework
Pesticide

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of parameters affecting the extraction procedure
Effect of modification ZIF-67 on the method efficiency
Selection of agitation type and time
Selection of type and volume of elution solvent
Type and volume of extraction solvent in DLLME
Comparison of the method with other approaches

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for financial support as the grant number of 70484 and the ethical number of IR.TBZMED.VCR.REC.1401.317.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of ZIF-67 (A and B) and ZIF-67-CDs composite (C and D), TEM image of ZIF-67-CDs composite (E), FTIR spectrum of ZIF-67-CDs composite (F), and XRD pattern of ZIF-67-CDs composite (G).

Study of effective parameters of DSPE step. (A) Study of modification of ZIF-67 in the extraction of the analytes. Extraction conditions: sample, 5 mL deionized water spiked with the analytes at the concentration of 500 ng mL−1 (each analyte); sorbent amount, 50 mg; adsorption time, 5 min; elution solvent type (volume), acetonitrile (1 mL); agitation mode, vortexing; desorption time, 5 min; and extraction solvent type (volume) in DLLME; carbon tetrachloride (25 µL). The error bars indicate one standard deviation of three repeated determinations. (B) Optimization of sorbent amount. Extraction conditions: the same as those used in Fig. 2 A, except ZiF-67-CDs was used as the sorbent. (C) Selection of agitation mode. Extraction conditions: the same as those used in Fig. 2B, except 40 mg was selected as the amount of ZiF-67-CDs. (D) Optimization of adsorption time. Extraction conditions: the same as those used in Fig. 2 C, except sonication was selected as the agitation mode. (E) Selection of elution solvent type. Extraction conditions: the same as those used in Fig. 2D, except 5 min was selected as the adsorption time. (F) Optimization of elution solvent volume. Extraction conditions: the same as those used in Fig. 2E, except acetone was selected as the elution solvent. (G) Optimization of desorption time. Extraction conditions: the same as those used in Fig. 2 F, except 1.0 mL acetone was selected as the elution solvent. (H) Salt effect. Extraction conditions: the same as those used in Fig. 2 G, except 3 min was selected as the desorption time.

Study of effective parameters in DLLME step. (A) Extraction conditions: the same as those used in Fig. 2H, except 2.5% (w/v) sodium chloride was dissolved in aqueous solution. (B) Extraction conditions: the same as those used in Fig. 3A, except 1,1,2-trichloro 1,2,2-trifluoroethane was selected as the extraction solvent in the DLLME step.

GC–FID chromatograms of standard solution (500 mg L−1) and blank of barberries juice sample after performing the method. Peaks identification: 1) diazinon, 2) haloxyfop methyl ester, 3) hexaconazole, 4) diniconazole, 5) tebuconazole, and 6) triticonazole.
Analytical figures of merit of the developed method.

ce:list-item.ce:label: a)
ce:list-item.ce:para: Relative standard deviation at a concentration of 5 ng mL−1 (each analyte)

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Standard deviation obtained from three determinations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the developed method with other approaches.

row.entry: Propiconazole, flusilazole, hexaconazole, fenbuconazole, penconazole, tebuconazole, and diniconazole
(Liu, Tian, Lu, Shi, Li, Gao, et al., 2021)
(Sun, Sun, Feng, Feng, Fan, Sun, et al., 2022)
Flusilazole, fenbuconazole, myclobutanil, penconazole and epoxiconazole
(Liu, Li, Huang, Zheng, Xu, Xu, et al., 2018)
Diazinon, haloxyfop methyl ester, hexaconazole, diniconazole, tebuconazole, and triticonazole

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: g)Magnetic dispersive solid phase extraction-high performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry.
h)Dispersive solid phase extraction-dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction-gas chromatography-mass spectrometry.
i)Pipet tip solid phase extraction- gas chromatography-flame ionization detection.
j)Solid phase microextraction-gas chromatography-electron capture detector.

head.ce:title: Application of ZIF-67 coated by N-doped carbon dots in dispersive solid phase extraction of several pesticides from fruit juices and its combination with dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction followed by GC-FID

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Food and Drug Safety Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dispersive solid phase extraction procedure was established for the extraction of some pesticides from fruit juices and then it was combined with dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction for more enrichment of the analytes. For this purpose, 40 mg of zeolitic imidazolate framework-carbon dot composite was dispersed into the sample solution as the sorbent via vortex agitation. After that, the sorbent was collected by centrifugation and the supernatant was wasted. Then, 1 mL of a water-soluble organic solvent (acetone) was added onto the sorbent as the elution solvent. After desorption of the analytes from the sorbent surface, the eluate was mixed with a water-immiscible organic solvent (1,1,2-trichloro-1,2,2 trifluoroethane) at µL-level (45 µL) and the mixture was quickly injected into 5 mL deionized water. The resulting cloudy mixture was centrifuged and 1 µL of the sedimented phase was injected into a gas chromatography-flame ionization detection system. After the optimization of the method, its validation was evaluated. The data showed that the limits of detection and quantification obtained by this method were in the ranges of 0.22–0.72 and 0.73–2.40 ng mL−1, respectively. The plotted calibration curves were linear in the range of 0.73–1000 µg L−1 for all analytes. High enrichment factors (375−445), and extraction recoveries (75–89%), and negligible matrices effect were obtained for the investigated analytes by the introduced method. Also, the obtained data showed that all samples were free of the analytes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) are a class of metal organic frameworks (MOFs) with outstanding and unique properties containing different porous pores (Dlamini et al., 2023). They are mainly prepared from Zn and Co cations (Ding et al., 2019). A large number of ZIFs have zeolite-type tetrahedral topology in which the M-Im-M angle is similar to Si–O–Si angle (Duan et al., 2022). Comparison of ZIFs with classical zeolites illustrated that they have high thermal and chemical stability and permanent porosity (Ajel and Al-Nayili, 2023; Akbari et al., 2022; Rangnekar et al., 2015; Yilmaz et al., 2012) and these advantages lead to the use of them in different applications like gas storage, catalysis, adsorption procedures, etc (Almasoudi and Mokaya, 2014; Duan et al., 2017; Hao et al., 2021; Ighalo et al., 2022; Torad et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2020; Zanon and Verpoort, 2017). Up to now, more than 150 types of ZIF-based MOFs have been introduced and received significant attraction in the researche (Ghalkhani et al., 2022; Zhong et al., 2018). Recently, different methods were extended to the synthesis of ZIF-67 and its derivatives such as solvothermal, sol-gel, and microwave/surfactant/ultrasound-assisted approaches. It should be noted that by the use of the mentioned methods, high-quality ZIF-67 is synthesized and the crystal size and its porosity can be controlled using temperature and synthesis time (Al-nayili and Muhammad, 2023; Li et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2022). In addition, the ZIF-67-based MOFs combined with carbon can create composites with some novel characteristics (Al-Abidy and Al-Nayili, 2023; Du et al., 2021). On the other hand, ZIF-67 is not enough hydrophilic to easily disperse into the aqueous solution. The addition of an organic component or its modification, makes it more stable in aqueous media. A large portion of modification is the addition of functional groups with various polarities and symmetries to alter the ZIFs adsorption ability without changing the transitional-metal cations. They are also more robust than other MOFs, which makes them more suitable for industrial applications. Furthermore, ZIFs exhibit less water uptake and have a more uniform pores size distribution compared to other MOFs. Therefore, ZIF-67 as the sorbent in solid phase extraction can be a good choice for the extraction of different compounds from complex matrices (Dai et al., 2018; Feng et al., 2021). Dispersive solid phase extraction (DSPE) is a routine approach for the use of MOFs during extraction procedures. In DSPE, a proper amount of MOF or sorbent is dispersed into the sample solution by a mechanical shaker (Ghorbani et al., 2020; Zahiri et al., 2020). Then, the dispersed particles were collected from the solution through centrifugation or by an external magnet (Chisvert et al., 2019). In the following, the adsorbed analytes are eluted by a suitable solvent to use in the following determination step or in other extraction methods. In recent years, the combination of the DSPE approach with another extraction method like dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction (DLLME) was performed to reduce the volume of reach and obtain high enrichment factors (EFs) (Xu et al., 2004). For this combination, the elution solvent used in DSPE should be a water-miscible solvent to utilize in DLLME as a dispersive solvent.

The major aim of the present work was to develop a DSPE method based on ZIF-67 modified by N-doped carbon dots (CDs). CDs are new carbon compounds which were firstly prepared through purification of carbon nano-tubes (Gbair and Alshamsi, 2022; Kar et al., 2021). They have very small sizes (∼10 nm) and a high surface-to-volume ratio. Furthermore, different functional groups containing oxygen are present in the structure of CDs. The presence of polar groups in the structure of CDs increases their solubility in aqueous media (Wei et al., 2015). Due to these properties, they have high adsorption capacity towards different compounds. Modification of CDs with other compounds and atoms like their doping with nitrogen atoms, improves their physical properties such as their interaction to adsorb polar compounds. The combination of ZIF-67 and N-doped CDs improves the dispersibility of the MOF into the sample solution and increases the DSPE step efficiency towards extraction of polar or semi-polar compounds (Mashkani et al., 2018). The studied pesticides in this study (diazinon, haloxyfop methyl ester, hexaconazole, diniconazole, tebuconazole, and triticonazole) are the common pesticides used by Iranian farmers (Pezhanfar et al., 2022). They have long chemical and photochemical half-lives and are easily transported to the environment and accumulate in agricultural products (Farajzadeh et al., 2022). The developed DSPE method is combined with DLLME to reduce the volume of extract to reach high EFs and low detection limits with a gas chromatography-flame ionization detector (GC-FID). Although performing DLLME increases the extraction time, very high EFs can be obtained by this combination.

Diazinon, haloxyfop methyl ester, hexaconazole, diniconazole, tebuconazole, and triticonazole standards with purity of more than 99% were obtained from Dr. Ehrenstorfer Company (Augsburg, Germany). Chitosan, glacial acetic acid, dichloromethane (analytical reagent grade), sodium chloride, hydrochloric acid (37%, w/w), chloroform (analytical reagent grade), sodium hydroxide, carbon tetrachloride (analytical reagent grade), 1,1,2-trichloro 1,2,2-trifluoroethane (analytical reagent grade), methanol (HPLC-grade), acetone (>99%), acetonitrile (HPLC-grade), dimethylformamide (analytical reagent grade), and ethanol (99.8%) were bought from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Co(NO3)20.6 H2O (Sinopharm, analytical grade), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) (Sinopharm, ≥ 99%), and 2-methylimidazole (Aladdin, 99%) were used in the ZIF-67 synthesis. Deionized water was used for the preparation of working solutions during the optimization and validation steps. It was prepared from Ghazi Company (Tabriz, Iran). Helium with a purity of 99.999% was bought from Gulf Cryo Co (Dubai, UAE).

The GC-FID system used for the determination of the studied pesticides was an Agilent 6890 gas chromatograph. The GC injection port and detector temperatures were adjusted at 300 °C during analysis. The extractant was manually injected into the injection port when it was worked in a splitless/split mode with 1 min sampling time. The analytes separation was done by an HP-1 capillary column with a length of 30 m. The column film thickness was 0.25 µm and its internal diameter was 0.25 mm. The initial and final column oven temperatures were 100 and 300 °C, respectively, and its temperature was increased at a rate of 10 °C min −1. They were kept for 3 min at 100 and 300 °C. The GC carrier gas was helium and its flow rate was 1.0 mL min−1. A Sartorius analytical balance ( ± 0.0001) (Göttingen, Germany), a 691 pH meter (Metrohm, Herisau, Switzerland), a BHG Hermile centrifuge, a FESEM MIRA3 TESCAC scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Welwyn Garden, UK), and a TV3U oven Shahla Co. (Tehran, Iran) were used during different steps of the method.

For the synthesis of CDs, a simple hydrothermal method was used. Initially, chitosan gel was prepared by dropwise adding 1% (v/v) acetic acid solution to 1 g chitosan (as a nitrogen and carbon source for the N- doped CDs) powder and the mixture was stirred for 1 h at 60 °C. One gram of the prepared chitosan gel was mixed with 10 mL of deionized water and the mixture was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave for 8 h at 180 °C. After this time, a bright yellowish solution was obtained and used in the following step (Kar et al., 2021).

ZIF-67 MOF was prepared by dissolving 58 mg Co(NO3)2.6 H2O and 908 mg 2-methylimidazole in 50 mL aqueous solution containing 1 mg CTAB. By adding 2-methylimidazole and cobalt nitrate, a purple precipitate was formed. After centrifugation of the mixture at 10000 rpm for 10 min and washing the obtained solid with ethanol and deionized water (to take away the possible impurities), it was dried at 80 °C for 4 h. In the following, 0.1 g of ZIF-67 MOF was mixed with the CDs solution obtained in Section 2.3 and the mixture was stirred for 2 h. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged at a rate of 10000 rpm for 10 min, washed with deionized water and methanol, and dried at 80 °C for 24 h.

For this purpose, 40 mg of ZIF-67-CDs composite was added into 5 mL of deionized water spiked with the analytes at a concentration of 500 ng mL−1 (each pesticide) or real sample solution, which its pH was previously adjusted at 6 (using McIlvaine buffer). Then, sodium chloride was dissolved in it at a concentration of 2.5% (w/v). The mixture was sonicated for 5 min and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min. After that, the supernatant was removed and the adsorbed analytes were eluted with 1.0 mL acetone. The elution step was accelerated by vortexing for 5 min. The eluate was collected by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 3 min and transferred into another tube. Thus, 45 µL of 1,1,2-trichloro 1,2,2 trifluoroethane was added into the elute and the mixture was rapidly injected into 5 mL deionized water by a 10-mL glass syringe. The obtained turbid solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min and 1 µL of the sedimented phase was injected into GC-FID.

Figs. 1A, 1B, 1C, and 1D are the SEM images of ZIF-67 and ZIF-67-CDs composite at different magnifications. The interconnected 3D porous structure with the rough pores is obviously observed. Also, a large number of crystal particles are anchored on the wall of the pores (Albdiry and Al-Nayili, 2022). According to the TEM image presented in Fig. 1E the nanoparticles distribution is regular and they are rhombic dodecahedron nanoparticles. The synthesized ZIF-67-CDs nanoparticles were also analyzed by FTIR and the obtained spectrum is shown in Fig. 1F. The bands at 1572 (CN), 1174 (C-N), and 992 (C-N) cm−1 are related to the 2-methyl imidazole used in the preparation of ZIF-67. The peaks at 3174 (O-H), 1640 (CO), 1422 (C-H or C-O), 1141 (CC), and 755 (C-O) cm−1 are the characteristic peaks related to CDs (Alwan et al., 2022). The recorded XRD pattern of the nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 1 G. The observed sharp and prominent peaks are indicative of the great crystallinity of nanoparticles. It is noteworthy to mentione that the recorded XRD pattern is in accordance with the previously reported papers regarding ZIF-67 (Abdulhusain et al., 2022; Sohouli et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters affecting the extraction procedure
Effect of modification ZIF-67 on the method efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned previously, the main goal of this study was the use of modified ZIF-67 MOF with CDs to effective extraction of the analytes from sample solution. It is expected that the presence of CDs increases the method efficiency compared to unmodified ZIF-67 MOF. To evaluate this, two aqueous solutions spiked with the analytes were extracted by ZIF-67 MOF and ZIF-67-CDs composite with the same amounts. The obtained data ( Fig. 2 A) shows that modification of ZIF-67 with CDs increases the method efficiency obviously. Modification of ZIF-67 with CDs enhances sorbent tendency to the compounds of interest via interactions such as surface adsorption, occlusion, van der Waals, and π- π interactions (Li et al. 2021). Thus, all of the following tests were done by ZIF-67-CDs composite.

The sorbent amount can change the method efficiency due to altering the accessible adsorption sites for the extraction of the analytes. By increasing the sorbent amount, the ratio of sorbent weight to solution volume increases and the method efficiency can be enhanced. It is expectable that dispersion of the sorbent particles into the sample solution at high amounts can be done hardy. To investigate the amount of sorbent, a series of experiments were done with the amounts of 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 mg of ZIF-67-CDs composite by keeping the other experiments constant. The experimental results (Fig. 2B) show that the method efficiency increases till 40 mg and then reaches to a constant value by exceeding the sorbent amount from 40 mg. It can be concluded that 40 mg of the sorbent has sufficient sites to adsorb the analytes (Abbasalizadeh et al. 2022a, 2022b). Therefore, 40 mg of ZIF-67-CDs was used for further tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of agitation type and time
ce:section.ce:para: In DSPE, the agitation mode of the mixture of sample solution and sorbent may have a crucial effect on the method efficiency. Usually, the sorbent and sample solution mixture is mixed by vortexing or sonication. Due to the different mechanisms of these agitations, the shaking type can change the method's efficiency. In this method, two different tests were done by sonication and vortexing and higher extraction recoveries (ERs) for the analytes were obtained by sonication compared to vortexing (Fig. 2 C). It can be related to the better dispersion of the sorbent into the solution by sonication (Marzi Khosrowshahi et al. 2022). So, the future experiments were done by sonication.

Optimization of adsorption time is an important factor in the DSPE step. When the adsorption time is insufficient, extraction of the analytes is not performed quantitatively. On the other hand, more adsorption time can result to back extraction of the analytes into the solution. In order to optimize the adsorption time, the experiments were done by altering sonication time in the range of 1–7 min. The experimental data (Fig. 2D) show that increasing sonication time increases the method efficiency until 5 min and then reaches a constant value. It can be related to the fact that 5 min is sufficient time for the adsorption of the analytes onto the sorbent surface (Marzi Khosrowshahi et al. 2022). Therefore, 5 min was selected as the optimal adsorption time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of type and volume of elution solvent
ce:section.ce:para: Desorption of the analytes from the sorbent surface is an essential step in DSPE. Commonly, the analytes are desorbed by contacting the sorbent with a proper organic solvent or aqueous solution. Because of the fact that different analytes have various solubility in solvents, the method efficiency can be altered by the use of different elution solvents. In this study, the elution solvent was selected among the organic solvents that are miscible with the aqueous solution due to the fact that they will be used as the disperser solvents in the following DLLME step. Subsequently, the adsorbed analytes were eluted with acetone, methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, and dimethylformamide, independently. The results (Fig. 2E) show that acetone gives the best analytical signals compared to the other solvents used. The better efficiency of acetone as the elution solvent can be attributed to its better role as the disperser solvent in DLLME and more solubility of the analytes in it (Pezhhanfar et al. 2022). Therefore, the next experiments were done with acetone.

The selected eluent volume must be optimized due to its effect on the desorption of the analytes from the sorbent surface and their enrichment in the DLLME step. To optimize the elution solvent volume, several tests were done with various volumes of acetone (0.50–1.50 mL) by keeping the other extraction conditions a constant value. The analytical signals (Fig. 2 F) increase up to 1.00 mL and then decrease. Decreasing the method efficiency at high volumes of acetone can be related to the increased solubility of the analytes in the aqueous phase used in the DLLME step (Sorouraddin et al., 2022). Consequently, 1.00 mL was selected as the optimum volume for acetone.

In DSPE, selecting an appropriate time for the desorption of the analytes from the sorbent surface provides an efficient desorption of the analytes and increases the extraction efficiency. To obtain the optimal desorption time, the elution solvent and the composite particles were vortexed at different times and the results (Fig. 2 G) show that the best efficiency is obtained at 5 min and all of the following experiments were done at 5 min vortexing.

In the extraction methods, by adding salt and thus increasing the ionic strength of the aqueous solution, different effects can be observed. Increasing the salt may increase the viscosity of the aqueous solution and reduce the analytes' diffusion coefficients and extraction efficiency of the method. On the other hand, it decreases the solubility of pesticides in aqueous solution and increases their migration into the extractant. Due to the fact that these effects are two opposite effects, the effect of salt addition must be investigated (Yao et al. 2019). In this method, several tests were done by dissolving sodium chloride at various concentrations (0–10%, w/v) and analytical signals obtained for the analytes at each concentration were plotted versus sodium chloride concentrations (Fig. 2H). The data confirm the positive effect of salt addition to the aqueous solution at 2.5% (w/v) and it was chosen for the next experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Type and volume of extraction solvent in DLLME
ce:section.ce:para: The extracted analytes by the introduced DSPE method were more concentrated by the following DLLME procedure. For this purpose, the obtained eluate containing the analytes was mixed with a proper water-immiscible organic solvent and the mixture was injected into 5 mL of deionized water. By performing this step, the analytes dissolved in the eluate were concentrated into a few microliters of the water-immiscible organic solvent. It is clear that the nature of the used organic solvent has a direct effect on the enrichment of the analytes. To obtain high efficiency, several experiments were done in the presence of carbon tetrachloride, chloroform, dichloromethane, and 1,1,2-trichloro 1,2,2-trifluoroethane. Due to the different solubility of the investigated solvents in water, the initial volumes of the solvents were different to obtain a similar volume of the sedimented phase (10 ± 1 µL). Based on the data ( Fig. 3A), the highest analytical signals are obtained by 1,1,2-trichloro 1,2,2 trifluoroethane. It can be related to more solubility of the analytes in this solvent or its better dispersion by the dispersive solvent into the aqueous phase used in DLLME step. Thus, it was selected for the next experiments.

To study the effectiveness of the extraction solvent volume, a series of tests was done by using 1,1,2-trichloro 1,2,2 trifluoroethane at different volumes (45, 50, 55, and 60 µL). As expected, the sedimented phase volume increased from 10 to 26 µL by increasing the volume of the extractant in the mentioned range. The obtained data (Fig. 3B) show that the analytical signals decrease by increasing the extraction solvent volume. Thus, to obtain high analytical signals, all of the following experiments were done with 45 µL of the extractant.

pH can change the structures of the analytes or sorbent and thus can alter the method efficacy. To study, the effect of pH on the method performance, the pH of the aqueous phase used in DSPE or DLLME was changed in the range of 2–10, independently. The obtained data confirmed that the method's efficiency was high in the pH range of 4–8 in both steps. This finding is in good accordance with the literature (Pezhanfar et al., 2022). Thus, there was no need for pH adjustment because of the fact that the used real samples' pHs were in this range.

According to SANTEH guideline (ec.europa.eu/food/sites/food/files/plant/docs/pesticides), the introduced method was validated by calculating important factors including the limit of detection (LOD: signal (S): height of the analyte peak/noise (N)= 3), the limit of quantification (LOQ, S/N = 10), coefficient of determination (r2), linear range (LR), repeatability (relative standard deviation, RSD), EF, and ER. The calibration curves were plotted by preparing different solutions at eight concentrations including 3, 5, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500, and 1000 ng mL−1 (each analyte) and performing the method on them. All of the experiments were done in triplicates. According to the calibration curves, r2 values were ≥ 0.994 for all analytes. The calibration curves were linear in the range of 2.4–1000 ng mL−1 for all compounds. The LODs and LOQs were in the ranges of 0.22–0.72 and 0.73–2.40 ng mL−1, respectively in deionized water. However, by considering the dilution of the samples with deionized water before performing the method the LODs and LOQs were in the ranges of 0.88–2.2 and 2.9–9.6 ng mL−1. The RSD values obtained from intra- and inter-day precisions were ≤ 6.4%. The EF is defined as the ratio of the analyte concentration in the final phase (Corg) to the initial analyte concentration in the aqueous phase (Caq) according to the following equation.
(1) EF = Corg/Caq

The EF can also be calculated from the ratio of the slopes of the calibration curves obtained with the pre-concentration and without the pre-concentration method. Also, ER% is calculated below equation:
(2) ER% = (Vorg/ Vaq) × EF × 100

Where Vorg is the volume of the organic phase and Vaq is the volume of the aqueous phase. EF and ER% for the analytes were in the ranges of 375–445 in deionized water (93–110 in fruit juices) and 75–89%, respectively. The results are listed in Table 1 in details.

To evaluate the efficiency of the developed method in real samples analysis, different packed fruit juices including apple, grape, barberries, and pomegranate were tested as samples. All juices were purchased from a local store in Tabriz (East Azarbaijan, Iran) and packed in tetra pack boxes. They were diluted with deionized water at a ratio of 1:4 before analysis. The obtained chromatograms confirmed the absence of the studied pesticides in the studied samples. To evaluate the method's accuracy and matrix effect, all samples were spiked at three concentrations and the method was performed on them. To assess the matrix effect in real samples, relative recoveries were calculated. They were calculated by dividing the average analytical signals (n = 3) obtained in real samples with to average analytical signals obtained in deionized water spiked at the same concentration and multiplying the obtained ratio by 100. The data are reported as mean relative recoveries in Table 2. The relative recoveries ranging from 80% to 110%, indicate the high accuracy of the method in measuring the studied pesticides in the samples. Also, according to SANTE guideline (ec.europa.eu/food/sites/food/files/plant/docs/pesticides) matrices of the samples have no significant effect on the performance of the proposed approach since the obtained mean relative recovery values are in the range recommended by the mentioned guideline (70–120%). Fig. 4 shows the typical GC–FID chromatograms of standard solution (500 mg L−1 of each pesticide) and barberry juice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the method with other approaches
ce:section.ce:para: The developed ZIF-67-CD-based DSPE method combined with DLLME was compared with other developed approaches considering several parameters including LOD, LR, EF, ER, LOQ, and RSD. The analytical characteristics of the methods are listed in Table 3. With a brief comparison among the methods in which MOF was used as a sorbent in the extraction of pesticides, it can be seen that this method parameters are comparable with the others even in the case in which a sensitive analytical system such as HPLC-MS/MS was used. On the other hand, it should be noted that efficient sorbents such as carbon nanotubes and graphene oxide were used in the mentioned methods. In this work, the used separation and determination system (GC-FID) was a simple, cheap, and easy to use apparatus, and the reported sorbent was obtained with a simple modification and cheap materials. The high efficiency of the method can be attributed to the suitability of the used sorbent and also the combination of the DSPE method with DLLME. Although the reported method by Farajzadeh et al. (Farajzadeh et al., 2018) with the same strategy of combining DSPE with DLLME, had slightly better results in most parameters, but the better repeatability of the developed method can be mentioned as a prominent point.

In the present work, a combination of DSPE and DLLME was employed for the preconcentration and extraction of diazinon, haloxyfop methyl ester, hexaconazole, diniconazole, tebuconazole, and triticonazole from fruit juice samples before their determination by GC-FID. In this method, only 40 mg of ZIF-67-CDs composite was used as a sorbent. The porous structure of ZIF-67 provided a high surface for the extraction of pesticides. On the other hand, the use of the DLLME method after DSPE provided high EFs. This is a great advantage of the developed method because the detection instrument (GC-FID) is not enough sensitive at µg L-1 and sub µg L-1 levels. Based on the results, the method had low LODs (0.22–0.72 ng mL-1) and LOQs (0.73–2.40 ng mL-1), good precision, broad LRs (0.73–1000 µg L-1), high EFs (375−445), and satisfactory ERs (75–89%).

Mohammad Nazari Kalojeh: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis. Roghayeh Hassanpour Sabet: Formal analysis. Mohammad Reza Afshar Mogaddam: Supervision, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Preparing revision, Mir Ali Farajzadeh: Writing – original draft, Preparing revision. Elnaz Marzi Khosrowshahi: Supervision, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Mustafa Tuzen, Writing – original draft, Preparing revision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interestsor personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for financial support as the grant number of 70484 and the ethical number of IR.TBZMED.VCR.REC.1401.317.
